MissionCommands.Message: Welcome to the housekeeping lesson. Today we’ll cover the routine procedures that must be performed (some periodically) throughout the long duration of an Apollo mission to the Moon.
During the Campaigns and Historic Missions, the mission will instruct you when to perform some of these tasks, so you will not need to manually keep track of them during those missions.

However, during the longer Flight Plan Mode missions, Free Play and during Multiplayer sessions, you will need to manually take care of these tasks, usually by following a schedule provided by the mission.
It is therefore vital to learn these procedures, and to understand when and why they are executed.
These procedures are the Waste Water Tank Drain, changing the CO2 absorber elements, purging the H2 and O2 tanks, cycling the cryogenic tanks and charging the batteries in the Command Module.

Let us start with the Waste Water Tank Drain procedure. The primary power source during an Apollo mission is provided by the Fuel Cells. This produces water, and this water is fed into the potable tank, and eventually the waste water tank.
In addition, the Environmental Control System also picks up water from the cabin and leads it to the Waste Water tank. This tank will cotinuously fill up and will need to be drained periodically.
The procedures for this is found in the SYSTEMS: ECS -> WASTE WATER TANK DRAIN checklist. Feel free to reference this checklist.
First of all, set the H2O QTY IND switch to WASTE to monitor the Waste Water Tank quantity on the ACCUM PRIM/SEC H2O quantity gauge.
The right needle will now show the current H2O level in the waste water tank. This can be anywhere between 0% and 100%.
Toggle off the cabin SEATS for better manueverability.
On the Water Control panel, set the PRESSURE RELIEF selector to DUMP A. This will start to slowly dump the waste water tank.
Go back up to panel 2 and monitor the waste water tank quantity.
Press ROGER when this is at 15%. It is common to dump the tank down to about 15% to 25%.
To stop the dump, set the PRESSURE RELIEF selector to 2.
It is important to periodically monitor the quantity of the waste water tank. If it is above 75%, consider performing a waste water tank drain if time allows.

The next procedure is the CO2 filter absorber element replacement. When the crew breathes, CO2 is released into the cabin. It is critical to remove this CO2 as this is toxic. The CO2 is automatically filtered by the CO2 absober elements (cartridges).
To access the CO2 CARTRIDGES valves on panel 350, you must open the CO2 ABSORBER CARTRIDGE ACCESS PANEL cover. Click to open it.
You will see two doors marked A and B. Two absorbers are used to filter the air as part of the ECS, and each can last for about 24 hours. Only one door must be open at a time.

One of these filters must be replaced every 12 hours. After you launch and the Mission Elapsed Time (MET) passes 12 hours, you must replace absorber element A. 12 hours later (so 24 hours into the mission), you replace absorber element B. 12 hours later (so 36 hours into the mission), you again replace absorber element A. This is repeated during the entire mission.
A mission flight plan usually provides a replacement schedule, so if this is provided, follow this schedule, and if not provided, follow the 12 hour rule provided previously.
The CO2 gauge shows the current CO2 level (partial pressure), and ideally should be 0 - 7 mmHg. If it is larger than 9 mmHg, the CO2 PP HI caution & warning light will illuminate.
If this happens, replace the CO2 absorber elements even if its outside of the provided schedule.
Let us replace CO2 absorber element A (cartridge A). Open the handle on the A-cartridge door.
You will see the door open and the cartridge exposed. Use the [C] button to toggle the function bubbles and click REPLACE CANISTER. Press ROGER when done.
Close the door again.
Then close the panel cover.

Next we will cover the fuel cell purging procedures. Fuel cell purging involves increasing the flow of oxygen or hydrogen through the fuel cell to remove impurities, leading to a purer gas and more efficient fuel cell power generation.
A fuel cell uses oxygen (O2) and hydrogen (H2) to generate power and water. Both the O2 and the H2 purges needs to be performed periodically throughout the mission.
An O2 purge is usually done every 20 hours, while a H2 purge is usually done every 40 hours. Follow these purging periods unless a schedule is provided by the mission.
Let's start with the oxygen (O2) purge.
The Fuel Cell Indicator selector can be used to select what fuel cell to monitor.
Let us start with Fuel Cell 1. Verify that the Fuel Cell Indicator is set to monitor fuel cell 1.

The H2 and O2 flow rates can be monitored on the FUEL CELL FLOW gauge. The Fuel Cell Indicator selector is used to select what Fuel Cell to monitor, and is currently configured to monitor fuel cell 1. Take a note of the current flow readings.
Flip the Fuel Cell Purge 1 switch down to O2 to start an oxygen purge of Fuel Cell 1.
This will on many cases trigger a Master Alarm. If so, depress the Master Alarm button to silence it.
If you take a look at the FUEL CELL FLOW O2 gauge, you will see that the flow has increased (leading to this potential Master Alarm).
The O2 purge should last for 2 minutes (120 seconds). Use a stop watch or a timer to keep track of the purge timing. Wait 2 minutes and press ROGER when complete.
Complete the purge by setting the FUEL CELL PURGE 1 switch back to OFF.
This completes the O2 purge of Fuel Cell 1.
Let us start the O2 purge of Fuel Cell 2. Set the Fuel Cell Indicator selector to 2.
Then flip the FUEL CELL PURGE 2 switch down to O2.
Silence the Master Alarm (MA).
Wait 2 minutes and press ROGER when complete.
Then complete the purge by setting the FUEL CELL PURGE switch back to OFF. Next we will purge Fuel Cell 3.
Wait 2 minutes and press ROGER when complete.
This completes the O2 purge of all fuel cells. Next we will perform the H2 (hydrogen) purge. Ensure that the Fuel Cell Indicator selector is set to 1.
A H2 purge line heater is used to ensure that the H2 purge line isn't frozen. Set the H2 Purge Line Heater switch to ON (up).
The heater must be on for 20 minutes before performing the H2 purge. Wait or use timescale for 20 minutes, then press ROGER.
Then set the FUEL CELL PURGE 1 switch to H2 to start the hydrogen purge.
Silence the Master Alarm (if triggered).
Then notice the increased H2 flow rate.
A H2 purge should last for 1 minutes and 20 seconds (80 seconds total). Use a stop watch, wait for 1 minute and 20 seconds, then press ROGER.
Then complete the purge by setting the FUEL CELL PURGE 1 switch back to OFF.
Let us complete the H2 purge on Fuel Cell 2 as well.
Wait 1 minute and 20 seconds, then press ROGER.
And finally, let us complete the H2 purge on FC3 as well.
Wait 1 minute and 20 seconds, then press ROGER.
Next we will let the H2 line heater run for another 10 minutes. Press ROGER when you have waited 10 minutes.
This completes the O2 and H2 purge of Fuel Cell 1, 2 and 3. You can find the procedures for this in the SYSTEMS: EPS -> FC PURGE checklist.
Next we will learn about the Battery Charger. You can reference the procedures on the SYSTEMS: EPS -> Battery Charging checklist.

The Battery Charger is used to re-charge the Command Module batteries (Battery A, B and C). These are typically used to share load during high powered maneuvers (such as ascent and SPS burns), and during entry & landing.
It is important to remove the load on a battery while it is charging. To do so, we can use the Main Bus Tie switches.
We will now try to charge battery A. To ensure there are no loads on Battery A, we need to verify that the Main Bus A/C switch is set to OFF.
Placing MN BUS TIE switches at OFF completes circuit from charger to selected battery and disconnects battery from main bus.
If you were to charge battery B, then the Main Bus Tie B/C switch needs to be off, and if you were to charge battery C, both Main Bus Tie A/C and B/C needs to be off.
In addition, some circuit breakers needs to be verified open to avoid additional load. These are typically open but I will show you where they are next.
cb PYRO A BAT BUS A - open (verify)
cb PYRO B BAT BUS B - open (verify)
cb BAT C TO BAT BUS A - open (verify)
cb BAT C to BAT BUS B - open (verify)
When charging battery A or B, the respective BAT RLY BUS BAT A or B circuit breaker is opened to expedite recharge.
The DC INDICATORS selector is set to BATTERY CHARGER to allow the DC AMPS and DC VOLTS gauges to display important information related to battery charging.
The charger voltage is monitored on the DC VOLTS METER, and the current output is monitored on the inner scale of the DC AMPS meter.
Then the BATTERY CHARGE selector is set to A. This will start the charging of battery A.
The inner scale of the DC AMPS gauge should show a value between 0 and 3 amps, depending on how much it is charging.
When the DC AMPS shows a low value (close to zero) and the DC VOLTS gauge shows that the battery has a voltage of 37.5-39.5 vdc, the battery is fully charged.
Next we will learn how to stop charging the battery.
Set the BATTERY CHARGE to OFF.
Close the BATTERY RELAY BUS BAT A circuit breaker.
This completes the battery charging procedure.
Next we will vent the battery compartment.
Set the system test to 7-A.
The SYS TEST DC VOLTS gauge might show a high pressure (above 1.5 vdc). If so, the battery compartment needs to be vented.
To do this, open the battery vent valve.
Let it be open for about 5 seconds.
Then close it.
System Test 7-A should now show a value close to 0.0 (< 1.5 vdc).
We can use System Test 5-B to show the voltage of the battery relay bus.
Next we will show the procedures of cycling the cryogenic tanks.
The O2 and H2 substances in the cryogenic tanks will separate into layers of different temperatures (stratification), causing inaccurate quantity readings.
To prevent this, fans are used to "stir" the oxygen/hydrogen in the tanks. This is done periodically, during troubleshooting and before taking O2 and H2 quantity readings.
Let us stir the cryogenic hydrogen tank 1 (H2T1). Set its fan to ON.
Let the fan stir the tanks for about 60 seconds, and press ROGER when done.
Then stop the fan by setting it to OFF. AUTO is typically not used.
Let us stir H2 Tank 2 (H2T2) as well.
Let it stir for 60 seconds.
Stop the H2T2 stir.
That completes the stir of the two H2 tanks. Let us start the stir of the Oxygen (O2) tank 1 (O2T1).
Let it stir for 60 seconds.
Stop the O2T1 stir.
Start the O2T2 stir.
Let it stir for 60 seconds.
Stop the O2T2 stir.
Excellent, this completes the lesson.

Title: Lesson 19 - Housekeeping
Description: This lesson will cover the periodic tasks required during a full Apollo mission.
BriefingText:
In this lesson you will learn about the normal housekeeping routines and procedures required during coasting.
Most of these routines are done multiple times according to the Flight Plan or mission schedule.

The following proecures will be covered in this lesson.
- Waste Water Dump
- CO2 Filter Change
- O2/H2 Purge
- Battery Charging
- Cycle the Cryos
